one_line_takeaway: AEGIS erases diffusion-model concepts by optimizing an adversarial target at the concept class's semantic center and using a data-free conflict-aware gradient projection for retention, cutting adversarial-prompt attack success rates well below prior methods while avoiding their FID/utility collapse.
key_finding: On SD v1.4 nudity erasure (Table 2), AEGIS achieves ASR of 12.06% (P4D), 8.45% (UnlearnDiffAtk), 3.52% (Ring-A-Bell) with FID 17.43 / CLIP 0.303 -- versus prior best SalUn at 19.86%/11.27%/7.04% but a badly degraded FID of 33.62, and AdvUnlearn at 80.14%/64.79%/59.86%. AEGIS thus both lowers ASR and improves FID by ~16.09 over SalUn. On Van Gogh style (Table 3) AEGIS reaches 36%/12%/10% ASR (vs AdvUnlearn 58%/38%/30%). On Church object (Table 4) AEGIS reaches 28%/6%/8%; SH matches the 6% UnlearnDiffAtk ASR but with a catastrophic FID of 68.02 vs AEGIS's 19.06. Ablation (Table 6, UnlearnDiffAtk nudity ASR): full AEGIS 8.45%; removing AET jumps ASR to 52.11%; removing PR gives 9.93% (but relies on COCO retention data); removing DGR gives 26.24% with FID degrading to 19.84; fixing omega=1 gives 14.08%. The motivation table (Table 1) shows bolting AET onto existing methods drops UnlearnDiffAtk ASR from 76.05% to 26.06% (ESD) and 64.79% to 5.63% (AdvUnlearn).
core_contribution: AEGIS is a retention-data-free framework for robust concept erasure in text-to-image diffusion models that jointly improves robustness (resistance to reactivation of an erased concept via adversarial prompts) and retention (preserved utility on unrelated concepts). It has two components the authors introduce as their own. (1) Adversarial Erasure Target (AET): instead of mapping an erased prompt to a hand-picked fixed target (e.g. 'a photo' or an empty prompt, as in ESD/UCE-style methods), AEGIS optimizes a learnable target embedding (length m'=1) to approximate the semantic center of the erased concept CLASS, so erasure pushes away not just the single instance but semantically related variants (nudity -> naked/sexual/erotic/impure). (2) Gradient Regularization Projection (GRP): a data-free retention mechanism combining Parameter Regularization (an L2 penalty (1/2)||theta - theta0||^2 that replaces an auxiliary retention dataset) and Directional Gradient Rectification (DGR), which, only when the retention gradient g_r conflicts with the erasure gradient g_e (cosine < 0), projects away the destructive parallel component of g_r via a scalar lambda = -omega*<g_e,g_r>/||g_r||^2, with omega dynamically annealed 0->1 so erasure is prioritized early in fine-tuning.
threat_model: A concept-erased diffusion model is deployed to suppress an undesirable concept (e.g. nudity/NSFW, a copyrighted artistic style like Van Gogh, or an object like Church). The adversary issues adversarial prompt attacks (APAs) that optimize a prompt c* (subset of learnable token dimensions) to steer the erased model's predicted noise back toward the original model's response to the erased concept, thereby reactivating harmful/erased content. The paper evaluates against three concrete prompt-optimization / red-teaming attacks: P4D (Prompting4Debugging), UnlearnDiffAtk, and Ring-A-Bell. (P4D and UnlearnDiffAtk optimize c* through model gradients per Eq. 3; the body text does not state each attack's access model.) Defense goal: minimize attack success rate (ASR) while keeping FID/CLIP utility. The vulnerability the paper diagnoses is that erasing a single instance far from the class semantic center leaves class-level residual information (small d2 distance) that adversarial prompts near the semantic center exploit.
limitations: (1) Erasure effectiveness degrades markedly on SD v2.1 (nudity ASR1 rises to 35.92% vs 12.06% on v1.4), which the authors attribute to OpenCLIP's more entangled representations -- so robustness is model-version-sensitive. (2) The retention 'loss' is only an L2 parameter-anchoring penalty to theta0, a heuristic proxy for utility rather than a direct measure of preserved concepts; residual FID gaps remain (Church FID 19.06 vs base 16.70). (3) The two theorems assume the erasing loss is convex and L-smooth, which does not hold for a UNet, so they are illustrative rather than binding guarantees. (4) Main tables evaluate single-concept erasure; multi-concept/mass erasure is deferred to the appendix. (5) All evaluated attacks are pre-existing prompt-optimization/red-teaming attacks (P4D/UnlearnDiffAtk/Ring-A-Bell); robustness to adaptive attacks specifically designed against AET/GRP is not tested. (6) ASR is measured with automated concept classifiers, inheriting their detection error.

datasets_used: Stable Diffusion v2.1 (base model, OpenCLIP text encoder)
Erased concepts: nudity (sensitive), Van Gogh (artistic style), Church (object)
COCO Object dataset (used only for the AEGIS-w/o-PR retention ablation baseline)
Adversarial prompt attacks used as evaluation: P4D, UnlearnDiffAtk, Ring-A-Bell
quantitative_results: Nudity SD v1.4 (Table 2), AEGIS ASR: 12.06% P4D / 8.45% UnlearnDiffAtk / 3.52% Ring-A-Bell; FID 17.43; CLIP 0.303
Nudity SD v1.4 SalUn (prior best on ASR): 19.86% / 11.27% / 7.04% but FID 33.62; AdvUnlearn 80.14% / 64.79% / 59.86%
Van Gogh SD v1.4 (Table 3), AEGIS ASR: 36% / 12% / 10%; FID 17.25; CLIP 0.310 (vs AdvUnlearn 58% / 38% / 30%)
Church SD v1.4 (Table 4), AEGIS ASR: 28% / 6% / 8%; FID 19.06; CLIP 0.305 (SH matches 6% UnlearnDiffAtk ASR but FID 68.02)
SD v2.1 nudity (Table 5), AEGIS ASR: 35.92% / 26.76% / 12.68%; FID 17.08; CLIP 0.309 (best prior AdvUnlearn 74.65% / 58.45% / 35.21%)

Ablation (Table 6, UnlearnDiffAtk nudity ASR / FID / CLIP): full AEGIS 8.45 / 17.43 / 0.305; w/o AET 52.11 / 17.54 / 0.305; w/o PR 9.93 / 18.15 / 0.295; w/o DGR 26.24 / 19.84 / 0.284; fixed omega=1 14.08 / 17.31 / 0.308
Motivation (Table 1, UnlearnDiffAtk Pre-ASR / ASR): ESD 20.42% / 76.05%; AdvUnlearn 15.45% / 64.79%; ESD w/ AET 6.38% / 26.06%; AdvUnlearn w/ AET 1.41% / 5.63%
Headline claim: ASR reduced by ~5.31% on nudity and ~24% on Van Gogh under P4D and UnlearnDiffAtk
